title: Vehicle-to-grid explained: can your EV power your home?
description: How vehicle-to-grid works in the UK, which EVs and chargers support it, what V2G can save you each year, and how it compares with a home battery.
blurb: Your EV battery dwarfs any home battery. What vehicle-to-grid can do today, what it needs, and what it pays.
lede: The battery in a typical electric car holds 50 to 80 kWh. The battery on a typical home storage system holds 5 to 15. Parked on your drive, your EV is the biggest battery you own by a wide margin, and for most of its life it just sits there. Vehicle-to-grid (V2G) is the idea of putting that capacity to work: charging the car when electricity is cheap, then sending power back to your house, or to the grid, when it is expensive. After years of trials, V2G went properly commercial in the UK in 2025, and the first real-world numbers are now in.
short_answer: Yes, with the right car and charger, your EV can power your home. A 60 kWh battery holds roughly eight days of average household electricity use (about 7.4 kWh a day). The catch is compatibility: only a small number of EVs and chargers currently support two-way power flow in the UK. Where it works, cycling cheap off-peak electricity through the car can be worth roughly £300 to £650 a year, and the first commercial V2G tariff offers free home charging in exchange for grid access to your battery.

sections.h2: V2G, V2H and V2L: what the jargon actually means
sections.html:
Three related ideas get bundled together, and they are worth separating because they need very different kit.

  • V2L (vehicle-to-load) is the simplest. The car has a socket, or an adapter for its charging port, and you plug appliances straight in, typically at 2.2 to 3.6 kW. Many current EVs from Hyundai, Kia, MG and BYD already do this. It is brilliant for camping, power tools and riding out a power cut with an extension lead, but it does not feed your house wiring.
  • V2H (vehicle-to-home) sends power from the car into your home's circuits through a two-way charger, so your fridge, lights and broadband run off the car without you doing anything. Your meter sees less import, so your bill falls.
  • V2G (vehicle-to-grid) goes one step further and exports power from the car out through your meter to the grid, usually under the control of an energy supplier that pays you, or rewards you, for the flexibility.

In practice, UK commercial offerings combine V2H and V2G: the car covers your home's demand first and exports any surplus the supplier asks for. The supplier's software decides when to charge and when to discharge, much like the smart control on a smart EV tariff, just running in both directions.

sections.h2: What you need, and why most EVs still can't do it
sections.html:
This is the part that disqualifies most people today. A working V2G setup needs four things.

1. A car that allows two-way power flow

Most EVs on UK roads cannot discharge through their charging port, full stop. The early UK trials all used the Nissan Leaf, because its CHAdeMO port supported two-way DC power. Newer cars use the CCS standard, and two-way support there depends on a newer protocol (ISO 15118-20) that manufacturers are only now rolling out. The first car sold in the UK with V2G working out of the box was the BYD Dolphin in 2025, using two-way AC charging. More models are arriving each year, but you must check the specific car and model year. Never assume.

2. A two-way (bidirectional) charger

An ordinary home charge point only flows one way. Early bidirectional DC chargers cost several thousand pounds, which killed the economics for most households. The newer AC approach moves the clever electronics into the car itself, so the wall box can be close to ordinary smart-charger money. This is the change that made commercial V2G viable.

3. Export permission from your network operator

Because the setup pushes power onto the grid, your installer applies to your district network operator for approval (a G99 application), the same process used for solar panel installations. A good installer handles the paperwork.

4. A smart meter and a compatible tariff

The supplier needs half-hourly readings to bill imports and credit exports, and you need a tariff that actually rewards two-way flow. As of mid 2026 only one UK supplier runs a mass-market V2G tariff, with others in trials, so your choice of supplier is effectively made for you. Check current availability before buying any hardware.

sections.h2: The money: what is a battery on wheels actually worth?
sections.html:
The core of V2G economics is simple arbitrage. You buy electricity at an off-peak EV rate of around 7p per kWh, store it in the car, then use it in the evening instead of buying at the capped day rate of 26.11p. Some energy is lost converting in and out of the battery (a round trip of roughly 85 to 90 per cent is typical), so each useful kWh costs you about 8p and replaces one that would have cost 26p.

Here is what that is worth at different levels of daily use. "Energy cycled" means electricity your home draws from the car during peak hours.

Energy cycled per dayOff-peak cost to replace itPeak-rate electricity avoidedNet saving per year
5 kWh£0.40£1.31£331
8 kWh£0.64£2.09£530
10 kWh£0.80£2.61£661

At 26.11p per kWh (Ofgem price cap, July to September 2026) against a 7p off-peak EV rate, assuming an 88 per cent round trip. Checked June 2026.

For reference, the average UK home uses about 7.4 kWh a day, so the middle row is roughly a household running mostly off its car outside the cheap window. Octopus, the first supplier to launch a commercial V2G tariff (Power Pack), structures the deal differently: home charging is free, in exchange for plugging in regularly (around 20 sessions a month at launch) and letting the supplier manage charging and discharging, within a monthly energy allowance. Free charging on a car doing average mileage is worth several hundred pounds a year by itself. Terms, eligibility and supported cars change quickly, so check the supplier's current conditions and Ofgem's tariff guidance before committing.

One quirk worth knowing: at launch, the first V2G tariff was not available to households that already export, which ruled out homes with solar panels or a home battery. If you already have panels, or are weighing them up, read our breakdown of whether solar stacks up before assuming you can layer V2G on top.

sections.h2: Will it wreck the car's battery?
sections.html:
This is the most common objection, and the evidence is more reassuring than you might expect. Battery wear depends on how deeply and how hot you cycle the cells, not just how often. V2G discharge for a home is gentle: a house drawing 1 to 3 kW is a far lighter load than motorway driving, and supplier software keeps the battery inside a comfortable state-of-charge band rather than running it flat.

Research from UK trials, including work at the University of Warwick, found that smartly managed V2G cycling adds little measurable degradation, and in some scenarios can be kinder to the pack than leaving it sitting at a high charge level. Manufacturers offering V2G-ready cars warrant the battery for two-way use, typically alongside the standard cover of around eight years or 100,000 miles.

That said, a V2G battery does do more work over its life than one that only drives. If you keep cars well past warranty, factor that in. For the background on how packs age and what real-world data shows, see how long EV batteries actually last.

If what attracts you is cheap-rate storage rather than the car itself, a wall-mounted home battery does the same arbitrage job. The comparison comes down to capacity, availability and cost.

  • Capacity: the car wins by a mile. A 60 kWh EV is the equivalent of four to six large home batteries.
  • Availability: the home battery wins. It is always there; the car is not. If you commute by car, the battery is absent exactly when you need it least and present overnight when electricity is already cheap. V2G suits households where the car sits on the drive most days.
  • Cost: if you were buying the EV anyway, V2G hardware is a modest add-on, far less than a separate storage system. Buying an EV purely as a battery makes no sense.
  • Flexibility: a home battery works with any supplier and pairs freely with solar, while V2G currently ties you to specific cars, chargers and tariffs.

There is no need to decide in the abstract: run the numbers for a fixed battery in our solar battery storage guide and compare the annual figure with the table above. For many households the honest answer in 2026 is that a home battery is the dependable option now, and V2G is the better deal if your car, parking and supplier line up.

Vehicle-to-grid is real, commercial and live in the UK, and the underlying economics are strong: your car carries more storage than any home battery you would plausibly buy, and the gap between off-peak and peak electricity (7p against 26.11p at the current cap) is wide enough to pay you several hundred pounds a year for lending it out.

The constraint is compatibility. In mid 2026 the list of cars, chargers and tariffs that work together is short, so the practical advice is timing. If you are buying an EV in the next year or two, make two-way charging capability a line on your checklist, because it costs little to have and may be worth £300 to £650 a year to use. If your current car cannot do it, nothing here changes your maths: a smart off-peak tariff and well-timed charging still capture most of the savings, and a home battery can do the arbitrage without the car. V2G is not a reason to change cars. It is a reason to choose the next one carefully.

faq.q: Can my EV keep the lights on during a power cut?
faq.a: Usually not automatically. Most UK V2G setups shut down in a power cut, just as solar inverters do, to protect engineers working on the network. Backup needs dedicated islanding hardware, which is rare in the UK so far. What many EVs can do today is vehicle-to-load: plug an extension lead into the car's socket or adapter and run the fridge, router and some lights directly at 2 to 3 kW.

faq.q: Which cars support V2G in the UK?
faq.a: Very few, as of mid 2026. The Nissan Leaf supported it through its CHAdeMO port in trials, and the BYD Dolphin was the first car sold in the UK with commercial V2G support via two-way AC charging. More manufacturers are enabling it on new models through the ISO 15118-20 standard, but support varies by model year, so confirm with the manufacturer and your supplier before buying.

UK trial evidence suggests well-managed V2G adds little wear. Discharging into a home is a light load compared with driving, supplier software keeps the battery within a healthy charge band, and manufacturers offering V2G-ready cars cover two-way use under the standard battery warranty, typically around eight years or 100,000 miles.

faq.q: Do I need solar panels to use V2G?
faq.a: No. V2G works by buying cheap off-peak electricity and storing it in the car, so it pays its way without any generation. In fact the first UK V2G tariff initially excluded homes that already export to the grid, which ruled out existing solar and battery households. Eligibility rules change, so check the current terms.

faq.q: How much can V2G save per year?
faq.a: Cycling 5 to 10 kWh a day through the car, bought at around 7p off-peak and used instead of 26.11p peak-rate electricity, is worth roughly £330 to £660 a year at July to September 2026 cap rates. The first commercial tariff instead offers free home charging, worth several hundred pounds a year for a car doing average mileage.
